request.question: Who did A.L. "Doodle" Owens work with to write many songs for the singer of "He Stopped Loving Her Today"?

title: A.L. "Doodle" Owens
I Can't Believe That You've Stopped Loving Me
(I'm So) Afraid of Losing You Again
I Never Once Stopped Loving You (song)
Wine Colored Roses (song)
All I Have to Offer You (Is Me)
The Right Left Hand
Somebody Wants Me Out of the Way
George Jones
Fourteen Minutes Old
sentences: Arthur Leo "Doodle" Owens (November 28, 1930 – October 4, 1999) was an American country music songwriter and singer.

He had a long songwriting partnership with Dallas Frazier, with whom he wrote "All I Have to Offer You (Is Me)" (1969), "(I'm So) Afraid of Losing You Again" (1969), "I Can't Believe That You've Stopped Loving Me" (1970) and "Then Who Am I" (1974), all number-one country hits for Charlie Pride.
In the 1980s, Owens wrote many songs with fellow songwriter Dennis Knutson for George Jones and other artists.
"I Can't Believe That You've Stopped Loving Me" is a song written by Dallas Frazier and A.L. "Doodle" Owens, and recorded by American country music artist Charley Pride.
It was released in September 1970 as the second single from the album "From Me to You".
"I Can't Believe That You've Stopped Loving Me" was Charley Pride's fifth number one on the country charts.
The single stayed at number one for two weeks and spent a total of fifteen weeks on the country charts.
"(I'm So) Afraid of Losing You Again" is a song written by Dallas Frazier and A.L. "Doodle" Owens, and recorded by American country music artist Charley Pride.
It was released in October 1969 as the first single from the album "Just Plain Charley". "
(I'm So) Afraid of Losing You Again" was Charley Pride's second number one on the country charts.
The single stayed at number one for three weeks and spent a total of 15 weeks on the country charts.
"I Never Once Stopped Loving You" is a single by American country music artist Connie Smith.
Released in March 1970, the song reached #5 on the "Billboard" Hot Country Singles chart.
An album of the same name was released in September 1970 that included the song.
In addition, "I Never Once Stopped Loving You" also peaked at #17 on the Canadian "RPM" Country Tracks chart.
"Wine Colored Roses" is a song written by Dennis Knutson and A.L. "Doodle" Owens, recorded by American country music artist George Jones.
It was released in September 1986 as the first single and title track from Jones' album "Wine Colored Roses".
The song peaked at number 10 on the "Billboard" Hot Country Singles chart.
The song tells the story of a man who receives a letter from a caring ex-lover who asks him if he still drinks.
Unable to overcome his own shame to speak with her on the telephone, he sends her a dozen wine colored roses as a symbol that he still drinks.

The song became part of Jones' live show at the time, and when he would sing the line, "She asked if I had quit drinkin'," audiences would often cheer, mindful of how George had gotten sober himself in 1984.

In his essay for the liner notes to the 1994 Sony compilation "The Essential George Jones: The Spirit of Country", Rich Kienzle states, "If there were any doubters, 'Wine Colored Roses' proved Jones was a timeless superstar, even without stimulants."
"All I Have to Offer You (Is Me)" is a song written by Dallas Frazier and A.L. "Doodle" Owens, and recorded by American country music artist Charley Pride.
It was released in June 1969 as the first single from the album "The Best of Charley Pride".
"The Right Left Hand" is a song written by Dennis Knutson and A.L. "Doodle" Owens, and recorded by American country music artist George Jones.
It was released in January 1987 as the second single from his album "Wine Colored Roses".
The song peaked at number 8 on the "Billboard" Hot Country Singles chart.
The song is about marriage; the narrator rejoices at putting "a golden band on the right left hand" after several failed marriages.
Many interpreted the song as George's tribute to his fourth wife Nancy, whom he credited with saving his life and career.
Jones would not see the Top 10 again until late 1988.
"Somebody Wants Me Out of the Way" is a song written by Dennis Knutson and A.L. "Doodle" Owens and recorded by American country music artist George Jones.
It was released in April 1986 as the third single from his album "Who's Gonna Fill Their Shoes".
The song peaked at number 9 on the "Billboard" Hot Country Singles chart.

The song is very much in the tradition of George's previous hits "Still Doin' Time" and "If Drinkin' Don't Kill Me (Her Memory Will)" in that it is a hard "drinking song," but it's also a "cheatin'" song, with the narrator suspicious that "Somebody keeps paying my bar tab but the bartender won't tell me who."
Jones delivers a soulful, bluesy vocal over a stone country arrangement with a prominent, shimmering acoustic guitar.
The singer, who had been a notorious drinker, was sober by this time thanks to his wife Nancy, who had gotten his career back on track after years of mismanagement and missed concert dates.
George Glenn Jones (September 12, 1931 – April 26, 2013) was an American musician, singer and songwriter.
He achieved international fame for his long list of hit records, including his best known song "He Stopped Loving Her Today", as well as his distinctive voice and phrasing.
For the last twenty years of his life, Jones was frequently referred to as the greatest living country singer.

Country music scholar Bill C. Malone writes, "For the two or three minutes consumed by a song, Jones immerses himself so completely in its lyrics, and in the mood it conveys, that the listener can scarcely avoid becoming similarly involved."
Waylon Jennings expressed a similar opinion in his song "It's Alright": "If we all could sound like we wanted to, we'd all sound like George Jones."
The shape of his nose and facial features earned Jones the nickname "The Possum."
"Fourteen Minutes Old" is a song written by A.L. "Doodle" Owens and Dennis Knutson, and recorded by American country music artist Doug Stone.
It was released in June 1990 as the second single from his self titled debut album.
It peaked at number 6 on the "Billboard" Hot Country Singles & Tracks chart and number 5 on The Canadian RPM Tracks chart.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

You are the first-hop summarizer. Compress the supplied passages into a short, actionable intermediate state that lets the next agent know exactly what is present in the passages, what is missing, and what to fetch next. Produce a single `summary` string only.

Summary content requirements (concise: a few short sentences or clauses):
- First-hop evidence: one short paraphrase or a very brief quoted phrase from the passages that links the relevant entities or facts supporting any partial answer. Do not copy long verbatim spans.
- Bridge entity: explicitly name the person/entity mentioned in the passages that the second hop should investigate (use the full name or label exactly as it appears in the passages). If the question uses only a given name and the passages do not disambiguate which individual is meant, state clearly that the identity is missing/ambiguous.
- Missing fact: state in one short sentence which specific fact is required to answer the question (for example: winner name, year, birthplace, or another identifying detail) and confirm it is not present in the given passages.
- Retrieval directions: give one concise, ordered, actionable next-hop plan (1–2 steps). Prefer concrete target pages or query patterns, e.g. "open the named person's biography to check for [missing fact]" or a short query pattern such as '"<NAME>" birthplace OR "born in" site:wikipedia.org' or 'open a chronological list of <AWARD> winners and search for <NAME>'. Do not instruct the system to execute retrieval here — only specify what the next retrieval should fetch.
- Clarification: if ambiguity persists (e.g., only a given name is provided and multiple candidates are possible), include a short clarifying request asking the user to provide the full name.

Hard constraints and tone:
- Be concise and factual. Keep the same neutral, summary tone as before.
- Do not guess, infer, or assert any facts not present in the passages (no candidate answers, no years, no birthplace assertions, etc.).
- Do not ask the user for permission to search external sources. Give explicit next-hop retrieval directions instead.
- Output only the single `summary` field text complying with the above requirements.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict: Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

You are integrating newly retrieved passages (second‑hop evidence) with the first‑hop context to produce a grounded, post‑retrieval summary that preserves the facts the final answer generator will need. Produce a single field `summary` whose value is 1–3 concise sentences that: (1) directly state the answer to the question (e.g., where the studios/recordings were located) based on the available evidence; (2) if any passage explicitly names a production facility or studio and ties that facility to a specific locality (for example by giving the facility name together with a street, town, or county), include one short supporting sentence that paraphrases that passage to show the link between the facility and the locality; and (3) include one very short sentence or clause that synthesizes the context and the supporting passage to place the studios in the stated locality. Paraphrase supporting passages; do not include long verbatim quotations.

If the passages do not conclusively link a named studio to a specific locality, preserve and state that uncertainty rather than asserting unsupported specifics. Avoid headers, meta commentary, long quotations, extra sections, or inventing details not present in the provided context/passages. Keep phrasing compact, factual, and focused on the evidence needed to support the final answer.

Output contract: produce only the `summary` field (1–3 concise sentences) that integrates the first‑hop context with the second‑hop passages as described above.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, and `summary_2`, produce the field `answer` as follows. Use only the visible summaries (summary_1 and summary_2) as the evidential basis for the final answer; do not add external facts except where the instructions below explicitly allow a limited, well‑justified use of internal background knowledge. Always produce the final result inside the [[ ## answer ## ]] block and make that block contain only the minimal answer string required by these rules.

1) General minimal-answer rule
- If the summaries unambiguously support a single short answer (a short name, title, demonym, year, or single noun/phrase of roughly 1–5 words), emit only that minimal token sequence as the answer. Normalize the chosen substring by removing any leading/trailing whitespace or surrounding punctuation but preserve the phrase's internal characters and capitalization exactly as shown in the summaries. Do not add quotes, em dashes, braces, labels, trailing punctuation, explanatory clauses, or any additional text.

2) When both summaries agree on an identical short phrase
- If summary_1 and summary_2 contain the same short named-entity string (after lowercasing only for agreement detection), extract that common phrase, strip surrounding punctuation/whitespace, and emit it verbatim (preserving original capitalization) as the sole content of [[ ## answer ## ]] with no surrounding or appended characters.

3) Patterned question triggers (minimal formatting + deterministic selection)
- 'Which ... is from X, A or B?': When the question asks which of two named options is from a place and the summaries clearly indicate a single band/person as the answer, emit only the minimal band/person name supported by the summaries.
- Comparative membership questions (e.g., 'Does A or B have more band members?'): If the summaries clearly indicate which band has more members, emit only that band's name.
- Short common-name questions (e.g., 'What do people in the UK commonly call the location ...?'): If both summaries provide the same short common name, emit only that short name string.

4) Nationality questions
- If the question asks for a nationality/demonym and either summary_1 or summary_2 contains a demonym, emit only the minimal demonym token/phrase. Prefer the first demonym found in summary_1; if none appears in summary_1, use the first found in summary_2. Emit it verbatim (no added words or punctuation).

5) Two-performer composition specificity
- If the question asks for the "type of musical composition" used by a named act and both summaries use a generic word like 'song(s)', you may consult internal knowledge only to determine whether the act is a two-person (duo) group. If you can reliably confirm the act is a two-person group, replace the generic term with the single-word or very short noun that names a composition intended for two performers (for example, 'duet') and emit that single word as the minimal answer. If you cannot reliably confirm duo membership, emit the minimal noun exactly as it appears in the summaries.

6) Narrow assumed referent (single-name trigger)
- If the question supplies only a given name (e.g., 'Maurice') and the visible summaries explicitly mention a specific example with that given name (e.g., a named example appears in the summaries) and the summaries identify the requested fact (e.g., the award), then you may assume that referent and emit the minimal answer (for example, a single year) if you have sufficient internal confidence. If you do not have sufficient internal confidence, follow the normal clarification behavior (emit a brief clarifying question as the answer field) rather than guessing.

7) What to avoid (formatting and content prohibitions)
- Never add explanatory text, full sentences, comparisons, justifications, or provenance in the answer field. Do not append decorative punctuation (em dashes, extra commas, trailing periods), quotes, braces, JSON-like structures, leading newlines, or any characters not part of the normalized minimal phrase. Do not invent facts not present in the summaries except where the two narrowly scoped rules above permit limited internal confirmation (duo-confirmation and the single-name assumed referent). If the summaries do not unambiguously support a single minimal answer and you lack permitted internal justification, emit a concise clarification request as the answer.

Additional specific, evidence-directed selection rules (apply these only when their trigger conditions are met, otherwise follow the rules above):

8) Prefer an explicitly named immediate jurisdiction when resolving county/country wording conflicts
- Trigger: The question asks which country/sovereign-state a county or borough is part of (phrasing like 'county' and 'part of what country' or equivalent) and the summaries disagree or one summary uses a parenthetical membership note such as 'A (part of B)'.
- Behavior: If one summary contains a parenthetical of the form 'A (part of B)', treat the pre-parenthetical name A as the immediate administrative region referenced by the summaries and emit A (trimmed of surrounding punctuation/whitespace) as the minimal answer. More generally, when summaries disagree about a place, prefer the summary that directly names the immediate jurisdiction associated with the county (the subnational or constituent area named in the summary) and extract that short named phrase verbatim as the answer. Do not add any extra words or punctuation.
- Fallback: If neither summary clearly names an immediate jurisdiction or both are equally ambiguous, emit a concise clarification question per the base rules rather than guessing.

9) Size-answer simplification when both summaries agree with an inclusive qualifier
- Trigger: The question explicitly asks for a size (phrasing like 'what size' or 'equal to or smaller than what size' or equivalent) AND summary_1 and summary_2 are identical strings for agreement-detection (case-insensitive) AND that common string matches the pattern '<size> or smaller' or '<size> or less' (case-insensitive, allowing optional surrounding punctuation/whitespace).
- Behavior: Produce as the minimal answer only the '<size>' portion of the agreed common phrase by removing the trailing inclusive qualifier 'or smaller' or 'or less' and any immediately adjacent punctuation/extra whitespace. Emit the remaining size token (number plus unit) verbatim as shown in the summaries (preserve digits, unit spelling/capitalization, and internal spacing).
- Do not apply this edit when the summaries do not exactly agree, when the common phrase does not match the pattern, or when the question is not asking for a size.

10) Prefer identical parenthetical short-names when present in both summaries
- Trigger: Both summary_1 and summary_2 each contain an identical parenthetical short-name substring (a substring of the form '(' ... ')' that, once trimmed, is 1–5 words long, agreement checked case-insensitively).
- Behavior: Extract the parenthetical content (remove the surrounding '(' and ')', trim surrounding whitespace and surrounding punctuation) and treat that extracted short-name as the canonical minimal answer. Emit it verbatim (preserving internal characters and capitalization exactly as shown in the summaries) as the sole content of [[ ## answer ## ]]. Do not emit the longer formal name when such an identical parenthetical is present in both summaries.

Output contract reminder: The produced `answer` must be exactly the minimal answer string determined by the rules above (or a short clarification question when required). The [[ ## answer ## ]] block must contain only that string and nothing else.

response.hop1_query: Who did A.L. "Doodle" Owens work with to write many songs for the singer of "He Stopped Loving Her Today"?
hop1_docs: A.L. "Doodle" Owens | Arthur Leo "Doodle" Owens (November 28, 1930 – October 4, 1999) was an American country music songwriter and singer.  He had a long songwriting partnership with Dallas Frazier, with whom he wrote "All I Have to Offer You (Is Me)" (1969), "(I'm So) Afraid of Losing You Again" (1969), "I Can't Believe That You've Stopped Loving Me" (1970) and "Then Who Am I" (1974), all number-one country hits for Charlie Pride.  In the 1980s, Owens wrote many songs with fellow songwriter Dennis Knutson for George Jones and other artists.

I Can't Believe That You've Stopped Loving Me | "I Can't Believe That You've Stopped Loving Me" is a song written by Dallas Frazier and A.L. "Doodle" Owens, and recorded by American country music artist Charley Pride.  It was released in September 1970 as the second single from the album "From Me to You".  "I Can't Believe That You've Stopped Loving Me" was Charley Pride's fifth number one on the country charts.  The single stayed at number one for two weeks and spent a total of fifteen weeks on the country charts.

Falling in Love Again (Marvin Gaye song) | "Falling in Love Again" is a 1977 song recorded by singer Marvin Gaye and issued on his 1978 album, "Here, My Dear" album.  The song was another track on the personal album that did not discuss the demise of his first marriage.  Instead of Anna Gaye, the song talked of the other woman in Marvin's life.  Described in "You Can Leave, but It's Going to Cost You" as "that young girl", she was Janis Hunter, whom Gaye had married.  In a solemn but still certain tone, he wanted to be sure that this time his love for Janis will be what he had always wanted.  But as irony would have it, by the time of the album's release, Marvin and Janis' relationship was failing.  By the end of the album's promotion, Janis had split from the singer after nearly two years as a married couple.  They eventually divorced in February 1981.  This song was the last song on "Here, My Dear" with a reprise from the album's "theme song", "When Did You Stop Loving Me, When Did I Stop Loving You", playing soon afterwards putting the album to a close for good.

He Stopped Loving Her Today | "He Stopped Loving Her Today" is a song recorded by American country music artist George Jones.  It has been named in several surveys as the greatest country song of all time.  It was released in April 1980 as the lead single from the album "I Am What I Am".  The song was Jones's first solo No. 1 single in six years.  The melancholy song was written by Bobby Braddock and Curly Putman.  The week after Jones' death the song re-entered the Hot Country Songs chart at No. 21.  As of November 13, 2013, the single has sold 521,000 copies in the United States.  Since 2008 it has been preserved by the Library of Congress into the National Recording Registry.

Put It in a Love Song | "Put It in a Love Song" is a song by American singer Alicia Keys.  The song, written and produced by Keys and Swizz Beatz, features guest vocals by American R&B singer Beyoncé Knowles.  It was originally sent to radio on January 19, 2010 by J Records, as the fourth single from Keys' fourth studio album, "The Element of Freedom" (2009).  However, the full single release was postponed, so that "Un-Thinkable (I'm Ready)" could be pushed forward as the fourth single.  The R&B and soul song, talks about a girl who just wants her lover to write her a song and prove to her he's worth her time.

The Yankee Doodle Boy | "The Yankee Doodle Boy", also well known as "(I'm a) Yankee Doodle Dandy" is a patriotic song from the Broadway musical "Little Johnny Jones" written by George M. Cohan.  The play opened at the Liberty Theater on November 7, 1904.  The play concerns the trials and tribulations of a fictional American jockey, Johnny Jones (based on the real life jockey Tod Sloan), who rides a horse named "Yankee Doodle" in the English Derby.  Cohan incorporates snippets of several popular traditional American songs into his lyrics of this song, as he often did with his songs.  The song was performed by James Cagney in the 1942 film "Yankee Doodle Dandy", in which he played Cohan.

The Bird (Jerry Reed song) | "The Bird" is a song recorded by American country music singer Jerry Reed.  Written by Hal Coleman and Barry Etris, this novelty song contains impressions of Willie Nelson's "Whiskey River" and "On the Road Again;" and George Jones' "He Stopped Loving Her Today."  It was released in October 1982 as the lead single from the album, "The Bird".  The song peaked at No. 2 on the "Billboard magazine" Hot Country Singles chart just before Christmas.
hop1_titles: I Can't Believe That You've Stopped Loving Me
Falling in Love Again (Marvin Gaye song)
Put It in a Love Song
response.summary_1: First-hop evidence: passage states Owens "wrote many songs with fellow songwriter Dennis Knutson for George Jones." Bridge entity: Dennis Knutson. Missing fact: none required to identify the collaborator — the collaborator's name is present in the passages. Retrieval directions: open the "Dennis Knutson" biography or songwriter credits (e.g., search "Dennis Knutson" site:wikipedia.org or "Dennis Knutson" George Jones songwriting credits) to confirm and retrieve the specific songs he co-wrote for George Jones.
response.hop2_query: Dennis Knutson A.L. "Doodle" Owens George Jones songs credits site:wikipedia.org OR site:allmusic.com OR site:ascap.com
hop2_docs: The Right Left Hand | "The Right Left Hand" is a song written by Dennis Knutson and A.L. "Doodle" Owens, and recorded by American country music artist George Jones.  It was released in January 1987 as the second single from his album "Wine Colored Roses".  The song peaked at number 8 on the "Billboard" Hot Country Singles chart.  The song is about marriage; the narrator rejoices at putting "a golden band on the right left hand" after several failed marriages.  Many interpreted the song as George's tribute to his fourth wife Nancy, whom he credited with saving his life and career.  Jones would not see the Top 10 again until late 1988.

A.L. "Doodle" Owens | Arthur Leo "Doodle" Owens (November 28, 1930 – October 4, 1999) was an American country music songwriter and singer.  He had a long songwriting partnership with Dallas Frazier, with whom he wrote "All I Have to Offer You (Is Me)" (1969), "(I'm So) Afraid of Losing You Again" (1969), "I Can't Believe That You've Stopped Loving Me" (1970) and "Then Who Am I" (1974), all number-one country hits for Charlie Pride.  In the 1980s, Owens wrote many songs with fellow songwriter Dennis Knutson for George Jones and other artists.

Swoopo | Swoopo was a bidding fee auction site where purchased credits were used to make bids.  Prior to changing its name to Swoopo in 2008, the website was called Telebid.  In March 2011, Swoopo's website became inaccessible, and a notice page claimed that Swoopo was experiencing "technical issues."  In February 2012, DealDash obtained the domain name for Swoopo.com.  The penny auction was invented by Lloyd Liske and William Buckell when the site was first created and known as telebid.com.  At that time the site received bids by phone and charged to transfer their bids to the internet site.  This is where the format of paying to place a bid started.  A lawsuit involving quibids.com and other major penny auctions was rumored to be coming and the site was sold to new owners.

Wine Colored Roses (song) | "Wine Colored Roses" is a song written by Dennis Knutson and A.L. "Doodle" Owens, recorded by American country music artist George Jones.  It was released in September 1986 as the first single and title track from Jones' album "Wine Colored Roses".  The song peaked at number 10 on the "Billboard" Hot Country Singles chart.  The song tells the story of a man who receives a letter from a caring ex-lover who asks him if he still drinks.  Unable to overcome his own shame to speak with her on the telephone, he sends her a dozen wine colored roses as a symbol that he still drinks.  The song became part of Jones' live show at the time, and when he would sing the line, "She asked if I had quit drinkin'," audiences would often cheer, mindful of how George had gotten sober himself in 1984.  In his essay for the liner notes to the 1994 Sony compilation "The Essential George Jones: The Spirit of Country", Rich Kienzle states, "If there were any doubters, 'Wine Colored Roses' proved Jones was a timeless superstar, even without stimulants."

Eric Olsen (writer) | Eric Olsen (born August 5, 1958) was the founder, editor-in-chief, and publisher of broad-based online critical magazine Blogcritics and author of local Cleveland blog Cleve-blog.  He is also a widely read and influential blogger.  His primary site, Blogcritics, has gathered together over 2,000 authors on a wide variety of topics and is a widely read news/information site with over 64,000 unique visits per day, and 50 new articles and reviews posted daily on a variety of topics.  In 2008, Olsen and his partners sold Blogcritics.org to media property Technorati.com and worked as the publisher and editor-in-chief to both Blogcritics.org and Technorati.com's newly re-launched original online media content site until September 2010.  On April 24 of 2011, he, along with writer Andre Morton and his partner/wife Dawn Olsen, launched TheMortonReport.com.

EverythingCU.com | EverythingCU.com is an online community of credit union professionals.  When it began in 2000, it was known as CUMarketingDept.com, and was specifically targeted to credit union marketing professionals.  In 2004, the name changed to EverythingCU.com.  As of November, 2007, EverythingCU.com hosts 5,620 members.  Online conversations cover a wide range of topics of concern to credit unions.  The site also facilitates document exchange among its members.

Review site | A review site is a website on which reviews can be posted about people, businesses, products, or services.  These sites may use Web 2.0 techniques to gather reviews from site users or may employ professional writers to author reviews on the topic of concern for the site.  Early review sites included first ConsumerDemocracy.com which introduced the helpfulness ratings, Hithen later Epinions.com and Amazon.com.
response.summary_2: A.L. "Doodle" Owens worked with songwriter Dennis Knutson to write many songs for George Jones (the singer of "He Stopped Loving Her Today"). For example, Knutson and Owens co-wrote Jones singles such as "Wine Colored Roses" and "The Right Left Hand."
